next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

The passage had been defeating him for three days.

Gavin sat on the sub-collection floor with his back against the shelves, the manuscript spread across his knees, his notebook open beside him on the stone. The room was cold in the way it was always cold — a dry, mineral chill that came from the limestone itself, unrelated to the weather outside — but in December his fingers stiffened faster and he had to stop every half hour to press them against his thighs until the joints loosened. His left hand tremored when it was cold. It tremored when it wasn't cold too, since the mass detention, but cold made it worse, a fine vibration that blurred the edges of the manuscript's notation when he held the pages up to the wall's glow.

The notation was doing something he hadn't seen before. The earlier sections — the ones describing the First Art's principles, magic as agreements that could be renegotiated, the substrate beneath spells — had used symbols that moved in pairs and small groups, showing direct correspondences between concepts. This section's symbols moved in larger patterns. Slow, layered, interconnected. When he translated one cluster and wrote the meaning down, the act of comprehension seemed to shift the patterns around it, as though the page were adjusting to his understanding. He wasn't sure if the adjustment was in the manuscript or in his perception. The distinction had been getting less clear for weeks.

He'd been translating the section using Babbling's framework — structural invariants, repeating patterns, mapping known elements onto unknown positio...
open_hooks_to_carry: The Cruciatus must be rendered with full physical specificity — the specific quality of pain without physical origin, the body's response to impossible signals, the aftermath of shaking and metallic taste
Gavin's wrong reaction: anger directed at lost work time rather than at the Carrows. This is the first beat of the novel's dark comedy and it must play deadpan, not winking
prev_tail_excerpt:
...and put her remaining toast on his plate. "Eat something. You've got Herbology in fifteen minutes and Sprout'll have you repotting Fanged Geraniums, so you'll want your fingers working."

He took the toast. He held it. It was cold and slightly stale and Miren's teeth marks were in the corner where she'd bitten off the buttered part, and it was the most ordinary object in the world, and underneath the bread and the butter and the plate it sat on the agreements hummed and shifted and held, the grammar of the world doing its quiet, constant, invisible work.

The first-year tried her spoon one more time. It rose two and a half inches, wobbling, and the girl pressed her lips together to keep from grinning and held the charm for a full four seconds before she let it down. She didn't look around this time. She was past caring whether anyone saw. She was practicing because the practice was working and she was eleven and magic was extraordinary and she did not know, could not know, would probably never know that a boy six seats down the table had just watched the grammar of the world bend around her delight and hold.

Gavin put the toast down. It had gone cold in his hand. He had Herbology in thirteen minutes and he was going to fail the practical on Fanged Geraniums and none of that occupied more than the smallest corner of a mind that had been quietly, irreversibly reshaped to perceive something no one else in this room could see, and the perception was not going to stop, and the toast was not going to get warmer, and whatever he had been before last night he was not that anymore.
state_deltas_required: The Carrow regime at its most direct: mass detention, the Cruciatus Curse used on students as collective punishment, and the physical aftermath that costs Gavin hours of work time

Physical (Cruciatus aftermath, persistent tremor in left hand) and relational (first real fracture with Miren over his apparent indifference to the torture). The comedy of scale becomes visible: Gavin's dominant response to being Cruciated is irritation at the lost evening of translation work
